# Conventions

## Design specs must verify function and table names against source before finalizing

[Trigger]: When creating a design spec that references specific database tables, API functions, or code utilities. [Detail]: Initial design specs may use placeholder or assumed function names (e.g., getSyncState). Before finalizing, verify actual names in the codebase via Grep or Read tools. Correct mistakes in the spec (e.g., getSyncState → getKgSyncState) before committing. [Consequence]: Finalizing specs with incorrect function names causes implementation to fail with MODULE_NOT_FOUND or undefined function errors.

_Source: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-12-auto-evolve-activation-design.md`_

_Tags: `backend`_

## Implementation plans stored in docs/superpowers/plans/ with date-prefix naming

[Trigger]: When creating an implementation plan for a multi-step feature. [Detail]: Plans follow naming pattern `YYYY-MM-DD-<kebab-case-title>.md` in `docs/superpowers/plans/`. Each plan breaks a feature into discrete bite-sized tasks with file paths, code snippets, test patterns, and delivery order. Plans reference the corresponding design spec by date. [Consequence]: Orphaned plans without spec references cause confusion about whether a design is finalized. Plans without date prefixes become difficult to correlate with completed specs.

_Source: `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-12-auto-evolve-activation.md`_

_Tags: `backend`, `config`_

## Design specs stored in docs/superpowers/specs/ with date-prefix naming

[Trigger]: When planning a new feature or significant refactor for open-pulse. [Detail]: Design specs follow naming pattern `YYYY-MM-DD-<kebab-case-title>.md` in `docs/superpowers/specs/`. Date prefix sorts chronologically and prevents collisions. Each spec includes summary, architectural decisions, and pseudo-code. [Consequence]: Storing specs without date prefixes makes the most recent spec ambiguous. Specs in other locations become hard to discover in future sessions.

_Source: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-12-auto-evolve-activation-design.md`_

_Tags: `backend`, `config`_

## callClaude no longer accepts effort option in knowledge pipelines

[Trigger]: When modifying knowledge extraction (extract.js, scan.js) or adding effort-based tuning. [Detail]: The callClaude() function signature was simplified — the opts.effort parameter check and --effort flag push were removed entirely. Both knowledge_extract and knowledge_scan pipelines now invoke callClaude without any effort specification, relying on default Claude behavior. [Consequence]: Passing { effort: 'max' } to callClaude is silently ignored. If effort tuning is needed in the future, it must be re-added to both the function and call sites.

_Source: `src/knowledge/extract.js`_

_Tags: `backend`, `database`_

## Plan generation detail view conditionally renders UI based on plan_status state

[Trigger]: When viewing a daily review suggestion that has triggered plan generation and checking its progress.
[Detail]: The renderPlanSection function checks review.plan_status and renders different UI: 'running' shows a spinner with elapsed time, 'done' shows the rendered markdown plan content, 'error' shows error message with a retry button. This pattern enables async operation feedback without full page reloads.
[Consequence]: Future async features can use this conditional rendering pattern to give users immediate progress feedback while operations complete in the background.

_Source: `public/modules/daily-reviews.js`_

_Tags: `frontend`_

## Frontend polling cleanup must stop all intervals on module unmount to prevent memory leaks

[Trigger]: When a SPA module with polling (like daily-reviews.js) is unmounted due to navigation or route change. [Detail]: The frontend maintains an activePollers Map that tracks all setInterval IDs for active polling operations. On module unmount, all polling intervals must be cleared via stopPolling(id) called for each tracked ID. The unmount() export must clean up activePollers to prevent intervals from running after the module is hidden from the DOM. [Consequence]: Leaking intervals cause memory bloat, phantom API requests, and stale data being processed by deallocated event listeners.

_Source: `public/modules/daily-reviews.js`_

_Tags: `frontend`, `performance`_

## TDD RED/GREEN phases explicitly separated in task sequencing for Claude CLI features

[Trigger]: When implementing features that invoke Claude CLI asynchronously (plan generation, daily review extensions). [Detail]: The implementation follows explicit sequencing: write test file FIRST (RED phase), verify it fails with 'module not found', then implement the module (GREEN phase), re-run test to verify pass. Then write route tests (RED), implement routes (GREEN). This prevents silent failures where implementation files are missing. [Consequence]: Skipping the RED phase can hide missing modules or functions until route integration tests fail, wasting time debugging at the wrong layer.

_Source: `test/review/plan.test.js`_

_Tags: `testing`, `backend`_

## Fresh config reading implemented via getConfig() helper to avoid caching

[Trigger]: When a new feature needs to respect configuration values that might change between requests (plan generation model, timeout, enabled flag). [Detail]: src/lib/config.js exports getConfig() which reads config.json from disk on every call. Route handlers import getConfig and call it fresh per request: const cfg = getConfig(). This prevents stale config values if config.json is edited while the server runs. [Consequence]: Configuration changes take effect immediately without server restart when using getConfig(); stateless design enables runtime tuning.

_Source: `src/lib/config.js`_

_Tags: `backend`, `config`_

## Frontend polling state management uses activePollers Map to track intervals

[Trigger]: When implementing async task polling in frontend modules that may be mounted/unmounted (e.g., daily review detail page polling for plan generation completion). [Detail]: The module maintains a module-level activePollers = new Map() to store setInterval references by task ID. startPolling(taskId) adds the interval to the map, stopPolling(taskId) clears it. On module unmount, the unmount() lifecycle function iterates activePollers.keys() and calls stopPolling() for each to prevent orphaned intervals. [Consequence]: Without this pattern, switching pages while polling is active leaves orphaned setInterval calls that continue polling the API even after the module unmounts, wasting resources and confusing state.

_Source: `public/modules/daily-reviews.js`_

_Tags: `frontend`_

## callClaude timeout must be extended to 120s for plan generation with large prompts

[Trigger]: When plan generation invokes callClaude() with prompts exceeding 500 KB (handoff context from daily review can accumulate to 1MB+ with mature knowledge bases). [Detail]: The callClaude wrapper in src/knowledge/extract.js defaults to timeout: 60000ms. Plan generation passes opts: { timeout: 120000 } to callClaude to allow Opus adequate time to process large prompts. Without extension, the Claude CLI process times out mid-analysis on mature projects. [Consequence]: Plan generation fails silently with ETIMEDOUT and returns empty results if timeout is not extended.

_Source: `src/knowledge/extract.js`_

_Tags: `backend`, `performance`_

## updatePipelineRun helper required for tracking async operation completion

[Trigger]: When implementing async Claude CLI invocations that spawn detached child processes (plan generation, daily review extensions). [Detail]: insertPipelineRun() is called with status='running' at the start of an async operation. When the detached process completes, it must call updatePipelineRun(db, runId, { status, error, input_tokens, output_tokens, duration_ms }) to record final state. This allows polling clients to retrieve completion status and cost metrics. [Consequence]: Async operations that don't call updatePipelineRun will show 'running' status indefinitely and clients polling for completion will wait forever.

_Source: `src/db/pipeline-runs.js`_

_Tags: `backend`, `database`_

## Plan generation implementation follows 10-step TDD pattern across database, queries, routes, and frontend

[Trigger]: When implementing a major feature like plan generation that touches schema, queries, routes, config, and frontend. [Detail]: The 10-step pattern is: (1) schema migration + cleanup, (2) fresh config helper, (3) updatePipelineRun helper, (4) plan queries, (5a) extend callClaude timeout, (5b) write plan.test.js RED phase, (5c) implement src/review/plan.js GREEN phase, (6a) write plan-routes.test.js RED, (6b) implement routes GREEN, (7) config.json keys, (8) frontend renderPlanSection, (9) npm test, (10) server restart + smoke test. [Consequence]: Skipping steps leads to missing exports, untested code paths, or incomplete integrations.

_Source: `test/review/plan.test.js`_

_Tags: `testing`, `backend`_

## Plan generation routes return 202 Accepted with task ID for polling, not blocking response

[Trigger]: When POST /api/daily-reviews/:id/plan/generate is triggered from the UI. [Detail]: The route handler spawns a detached child process via spawn() with detached: true to invoke Claude CLI, captures the PID in pipeline_runs table, and returns 202 status immediately with {plan_id, task_id, plan_status: 'running'}. The frontend then polls GET /api/daily-reviews/:id/plan-status to track completion. This prevents blocking the Fastify event loop for 300-600 seconds while waiting for Opus to complete plan synthesis. [Consequence]: Not returning 202 and spawning detached causes the HTTP request to hang, blocking all API requests on the same event loop until Claude CLI completes.

_Source: `src/routes/daily-reviews.js`_

_Tags: `api`, `backend`, `performance`_

## getConfig helper reads fresh from disk per call, not cached in memory

[Trigger]: When adding new configuration parameters to features like plan generation that may be modified between requests. [Detail]: src/lib/config.js exports getConfig() which reads config.json from disk on every call using fs.readFileSync and JSON.parse. Do NOT cache the config object in memory. This allows config changes to take effect immediately without server restart. [Consequence]: Caching config in memory means configuration changes are ignored until the next server restart.

_Source: `src/lib/config.js`_

_Tags: `backend`, `config`, `convention`_

## Plan generation polling uses activePollers Map to track intervals and cleanup on unmount

[Trigger]: When frontend modules poll for async task completion (e.g., plan generation status) and may be unmounted before the task completes. [Detail]: The daily-reviews module maintains activePollers as a module-level Map keyed by task ID. startPolling(taskId) adds the setInterval ID, stopPolling(taskId) clears it. The unmount() function iterates all active pollers and stops them to prevent dangling intervals after navigation. [Consequence]: Without explicit cleanup on unmount, polling intervals continue running in the background, wasting CPU and preventing garbage collection of module state.

_Source: `public/modules/daily-reviews.js`_

_Tags: `frontend`, `convention`_

## Knowledge extraction passes --effort flag to Claude CLI

[Trigger]: When tuning knowledge extraction quality or cost via configuration. [Detail]: src/knowledge/scan.js now passes { effort: 'max' } when invoking callClaude() for the cold-start knowledge scan. This flag tells Claude CLI to use maximum reasoning and effort for knowledge synthesis. The effort parameter is passed through the opts object in the callClaude(prompt, model, opts) signature. [Consequence]: If you remove the effort parameter, knowledge extraction quality may degrade — entries become less comprehensive and more prone to duplicates.

_Source: `src/knowledge/scan.js`_

_Tags: `backend`, `config`_

## parsePlanOutput expects labeled JSON block format with triple-backtick fencing

[Trigger]: When the plan generation Claude CLI returns structured output that needs parsing. [Detail]: The LLM output format uses labeled ```json blocks with triple-backtick fencing: ```json {"steps": [...]} ```. The parser extracts content between the triple-backticks and parses as JSON. This format is more reliable than plain JSON and prevents the model from accidentally embedding JSON in prose. [Consequence]: Without labeled blocks, parser may extract partial JSON or prose that looks like JSON but fails to parse.

_Source: `src/review/plan.js`_

_Tags: `backend`_

## All Claude CLI invocations instrumented via pipeline_runs table with insert-then-update pattern

[Trigger]: When knowledge extraction, knowledge scanning, plan generation, and daily review invoke the claude CLI. [Detail]: Every pipeline (extract.js, scan.js, review/pipeline.js, review/plan.js) follows: insertPipelineRun(db, {pipeline, project_id, model, status: 'running'}) at start, then updatePipelineRun(db, id, {status: 'done', input_tokens, output_tokens, duration_ms}) at completion. This separates operation lifecycle (insert/update) from state tracking (pipeline_runs). [Consequence]: Missing instrumentation → no cost tracking → no visibility into LLM usage → can't diagnose failures or optimize spend.

_Source: `src/db/pipeline-runs.js`_

_Tags: `backend`, `database`_

## Plan generation spawns detached child process to unblock HTTP response

[Trigger]: When designing async Claude CLI invocations that risk blocking the Fastify event loop (300-600 seconds). [Detail]: Plan generation uses spawn() with detached: true and stdio: 'ignore' to launch plan.js as an independent background process. The parent route returns immediately with 202 Accepted + taskId, allowing other requests to proceed. The child process writes results to the database when complete. [Consequence]: Blocking execFileSync → all concurrent requests stall, timeouts cascade, client thinks server is dead.

_Source: `src/routes/daily-reviews.js`_

_Tags: `backend`_

## Plan generation implementation follows 10-step TDD pattern across schema, queries, routes, and frontend

[Trigger]: When implementing a major feature like plan generation that touches multiple system layers. [Detail]: The systematic pattern is: (1) schema migration + cleanup, (2) fresh config helper, (3) updatePipelineRun helper, (4) plan queries (savePlan), (5) extend callClaude timeout, (6) write unit tests (RED), (7) implement core logic (GREEN), (8) write route tests (RED), (9) implement routes (GREEN), (10) add config keys, (11) frontend rendering, (12) run all tests. Each step is discrete and buildable. [Consequence]: Skipping steps causes cascading failures — tests import missing functions, routes call undefined queries, frontend renders without backend support.

_Source: `src/review/plan.js`_

_Tags: `backend`, `testing`_

## Plan generation configuration keys in config.json

[Trigger]: When configuring plan generation feature or customizing how plans are generated. [Detail]: Three config keys control plan generation: plan_generation_enabled (boolean), plan_generation_model (string, default 'opus'), plan_generation_timeout_ms (number, default 120000). The timeout accommodates large daily review contexts (1MB+ prompts). Model can be switched to 'sonnet' for cost reduction. [Consequence]: Missing or misconfigured keys cause plan generation to use defaults; timeout too short causes failures on large prompts.

_Source: `config.json`_

_Tags: `config`_

## callClaude timeout must be extended for plan generation with large prompts

[Trigger]: When plan generation invokes callClaude() with prompts exceeding 500 KB (handoff context from daily review). [Detail]: The callClaude timeout was increased from the default (60s) to 120s (plan_generation_timeout_ms in config.json). This accommodates Opus processing of large daily review context (1MB+ assembled prompts) and complex implementation planning. [Consequence]: With default 60s timeout, plan generation on mature projects (39+ sessions, large knowledge base) would timeout prematurely, leaving plan_status='running' forever.

_Source: `src/knowledge/extract.js`_

_Tags: `config`, `backend`_

## All three LLM pipelines instrument calls via pipeline_runs table with insert-then-update pattern

[Trigger]: When knowledge extraction, knowledge scanning, and daily review invoke the claude CLI. [Detail]: All three pipelines follow the same pattern: insertPipelineRun(db, { pipeline, project_id, model, status: 'running' }) at start, then updatePipelineRun(db, runId, { status, input_tokens, output_tokens, duration_ms }) on completion. This tracks internal LLM costs separately from user event costs. [Consequence]: Without instrumentation, internal operation costs are invisible; developers cannot optimize LLM usage for features like plan generation.

_Source: `src/db/pipeline-runs.js`_

_Tags: `database`, `backend`_

## Plan generation implementation follows 10-step systematic pattern

[Trigger]: When implementing a major feature like plan generation that touches multiple system layers. [Detail]: The pattern spans 10 sequential steps: (1) schema migration, (2) fresh config helper, (3) updatePipelineRun helper, (4) plan queries, (5a-c) extend callClaude timeout + write tests + implement module, (6a-b) write route tests + implement routes, (7) config keys, (8) frontend rendering, (9) test suite, (10) server restart + smoke test. Each step builds on the previous, preventing skipped tasks or out-of-order dependencies. [Consequence]: Skipping or reordering steps leads to missing test suites, untested routes, or frontend components that call non-existent APIs.

_Tags: `backend`, `testing`_

## Plan generation test pattern verifies both unit (parsePlanOutput, buildPlanPrompt) and integration (async spawn, status updates)

[Trigger]: When writing tests for plan generation feature spanning src/review/plan.js and routes. [Detail]: test/review/plan.test.js contains RED phase tests for helper functions (parsePlanOutput, buildPlanPrompt, selectCandidateFiles). test/routes/plan-routes.test.js contains integration tests for the POST /api/daily-reviews/:id/generate-plan endpoint, verifying the spawned process updates pipeline_runs and daily_reviews tables. The split allows unit tests to run fast while integration tests verify the full async machinery. [Consequence]: Merging all tests into one file makes it hard to diagnose whether failures are in plan logic or async spawning—separation of concerns is critical for async feature testing.

_Source: `test/review/plan.test.js`_

_Tags: `testing`, `backend`_

## Plan generation route integration returns 202 Accepted with task ID for polling, not blocking response

[Trigger]: When POST /api/daily-reviews/:id/generate-plan is triggered from the UI. [Detail]: The route handler calls generatePlan() which spawns a detached child process and returns immediately with a task ID. The HTTP response is 202 Accepted, not 200 OK. The frontend polls GET /api/daily-reviews/:id to check plan_status field transitioning from 'pending' to 'done'. [Consequence]: Returning 200 instead of 202 signals request completion when the operation is still running in background—frontend won't poll and plan results won't be discovered.

_Source: `src/routes/daily-reviews.js`_

_Tags: `api`, `frontend`_

## Design specs stored in docs/superpowers/specs/ with date prefix

[Trigger]: When planning a new feature or significant refactor for open-pulse. [Detail]: Design specification documents are stored in docs/superpowers/specs/ with a date-prefix filename format (YYYY-MM-DD-descriptive-name.md). This co-locates design planning with implementation and makes it easy to track when decisions were made. Each spec is a standalone markdown document that outlines the problem, approach, stages, and implementation checklist. [Consequence]: Specs serve as handoff documentation and future reference; omitting the date prefix makes specs harder to discover in chronological order.

_Source: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-12-auto-evolve-activation-design.md`_

_Tags: `backend`, `config`_

## TDD RED/GREEN phases explicitly separated in task sequencing for plan generation

[Trigger]: When implementing async Claude CLI features that require both unit tests and route tests. [Detail]: Step 5 writes tests first (RED), then step 5 implements the module (GREEN) without touching tests. Step 6a writes route tests (RED), then 6b implements routes (GREEN). This separation ensures tests genuinely fail before implementation. [Consequence]: Mixing test and implementation in the same step risks invalid tests that pass without real implementation — skips RED phase discipline.

_Source: `test/review/plan.test.js`_

_Tags: `testing`, `backend`_

## Test imports must be updated when backend functions are removed

[Trigger]: When a database query function is deleted from a module like src/db/projects.js (e.g., getProjectTimeline). [Detail]: The test file imports the removed function at the top (const { getProjectTimeline } = require('...')). Failure to remove this import causes a module not found error that blocks all tests from running. The import statement must be updated to remove the deleted function name from the destructuring pattern. [Consequence]: Tests fail to load, blocking verification of remaining functionality and causing CI to fail.

_Source: `test/routes/learning.test.js`_

_Tags: `testing`, `backend`_

## Syntax validation required after JavaScript module edits

[Trigger]: After making edits to JavaScript files across multiple modules (routes, database, frontend). [Detail]: Use `node --check <file>` to validate syntax for each modified file before running tests. This catches typos, missing commas, unclosed braces that would otherwise cause runtime errors. Quick validation prevents cascading failures across tests. Timeline removal touched 4 files (projects.js frontend, projects.js routes, projects.js database, learning.test.js) — each was syntax-checked immediately after editing. [Consequence]: Syntax errors in one file cascade to break multiple tests and delay feedback; immediate validation catches errors before running expensive test suites.

_Tags: `testing`, `backend`, `frontend`_

## Feature removal verification includes syntax, tests, API, and screenshot checks

[Trigger]: When verifying that a deprecated feature has been cleanly removed without breaking other functionality. [Detail]: Use a multi-layer verification workflow: (1) Syntax validation with `node --check` on modified files; (2) Full test suite with `npm test` to ensure no regressions; (3) API health checks with `curl` to verify endpoints (404 for deleted, 200 for remaining); (4) Playwright screenshots to visually confirm the UI no longer renders the removed component (Timeline card should not appear on project detail page). This 4-layer approach catches both code-level errors and visual regressions. [Consequence]: Skipping any layer allows regressions to slip through — deleted endpoints may still be called, removed UI components may still render, or tests may pass while the actual page is broken.

_Tags: `testing`, `frontend`, `backend`_

## API endpoint removal requires updating CLAUDE.md endpoint table and text

[Trigger]: When a REST API endpoint is deleted from src/routes/ (e.g., GET /api/projects/:id/timeline). [Detail]: Two independent locations in CLAUDE.md must be updated: (1) the API endpoint table under 'Endpoints' section listing method, path, purpose; (2) descriptive text in the architecture section mentioning the feature (e.g., 'Projects list + detail' changed from 'Projects list + detail (timeline, session breakdown)' to 'Projects list + detail (session breakdown)'). Missing either update leaves stale documentation that contradicts the code. [Consequence]: Developers follow outdated CLAUDE.md and attempt to call deleted endpoints, wasting time debugging 404 errors.

_Source: `CLAUDE.md`_

_Tags: `backend`_

## Vault files regenerate only when kg_vault_hashes rows are deleted first

[Trigger]: After marking knowledge entries as outdated via SQL UPDATE status='outdated'. [Detail]: The renderKnowledgeVault(db, projectId) function uses content hashing to skip unchanged files. To force regeneration after updating entries, delete the corresponding kg_vault_hashes rows for the affected categories (e.g., 'footguns.md'). Then call renderKnowledgeVault again to rebuild the markdown files from the updated knowledge_entries table. [Consequence]: Without deleting the hash rows, vault files remain stale with outdated content; the hashes prevent regeneration even though the underlying entries changed.

_Source: `src/knowledge/vault.js`_

_Tags: `backend`, `database`_

## Mark knowledge entries outdated rather than delete when deprecating features

[Trigger]: When a feature is removed from the codebase and has accumulated related entries in the knowledge base (25+ in Timeline case). [Detail]: Instead of deleting knowledge entries, use SQL UPDATE to mark them status='outdated'. This preserves deletion history and prevents data loss. After marking outdated, delete the corresponding kg_vault_hashes rows and regenerate the vault files to update the markdown exports. Bulk updates use LIKE patterns on title and body fields to catch all related entries. [Consequence]: Deleting entries causes silent data loss and breaks the audit trail; marking outdated preserves history while removing stale content from live documentation.

_Source: `src/knowledge/vault.js`_

_Tags: `database`, `backend`_

## Dead code removal requires coordinating three layers: frontend, routes, database

[Trigger]: When systematically removing a deprecated feature that spans multiple system layers (UI component, HTTP route, database function). [Detail]: Feature removal cannot be done in a single layer — the changes must be coordinated across frontend (projects.js), routes (src/routes/projects.js), database queries (src/db/projects.js), tests (test/routes/learning.test.js), and documentation (CLAUDE.md). Removing only the frontend leaves orphaned routes and exports; removing only the routes breaks tests. All three layers must be updated together. [Consequence]: Incomplete removal causes import errors, broken tests, or orphaned API endpoints that waste context and confuse future developers.

_Source: `src/routes/projects.js`_

_Tags: `backend`, `frontend`, `testing`_
